name: STEMI - Anterior
identifyBy: ST elevation >=2mm in 2 or more contiguous precordial leads (V1-V4)
Reciprocal ST depression in inferior leads (II, III, aVF)
Hyperacute (tall, broad) T waves may precede ST elevation
Pathological Q waves develop within hours - loss of R wave progression V1-V4
New LBBB may represent anterior STEMI equivalent

measurements.rate: 60-100 bpm (may be sinus tachycardia if in shock)
measurements.stSegment: Elevation >=2mm V1-V4; Depression II, III, aVF (reciprocal)

management: 12-lead ECG - confirm in 2 contiguous leads; repeat if initial ECG non-diagnostic
Aspirin 300mg PO (chew) - give immediately unless allergy/contraindication
GTN sublingual - titrate for pain if systolic BP >90mmHg (caution: RV infarct)
Morphine or Fentanyl - titrate for pain relief
Urgent evacuation - door-to-balloon time target <90 minutes
Continuous ECG monitoring - watch for VF/VT
pearls: Anterior STEMI carries the highest mortality of all STEMI territories - LAD is the 'widow maker'
Hyperacute T waves (tall, peaked, broad base) may precede ST elevation - treat as STEMI equivalent
Loss of R wave progression in V1-V4 indicates prior or evolving anterior infarction
Left main occlusion: ST elevation in aVR >1mm with diffuse ST depression - highest mortality pattern
De Winter T-waves (upsloping ST depression + hyperacute T) are a LAD occlusion equivalent without ST elevation
differentials: Early repolarisation (younger patients, no dynamic change)

qaNotes: Ischaemia/STEMI morphology retained from source content; verify affected leads in multi-lead view.
